summary: Seeded the research with five official-source candidates for the RAG evaluation tooling matrix: Ragas, DeepEval, Phoenix, LangSmith, and Promptfoo. Captured their practical positioning around metric-driven eval loops, local/CI testing, observability plus RAG eval, managed dataset/experiment workflows, and lightweight CLI-based RAG tests.
next_angle: Expand the candidate set with official docs/GitHub evidence for Langfuse, Opik, TruLens, MLflow GenAI, Giskard, Evidently, Weave, LlamaIndex, Haystack, and OpenAI Evals; then define compact scoring dimensions for the XLSX workbook.
open_questions: Which tools should be treated as primary matrix rows versus exclusions or caveats, especially general model benchmark frameworks and framework-specific built-ins?
How should commercial and open-source tools be scored fairly using only public docs, GitHub repos, and vendor pages?
What default weighting should the workbook use for a production RAG engineering team: CI/regression testing, RAG-specific metrics, observability, human review, self-hosting, or enterprise governance?

sources.title: Ragas documentation
sources.note: Official docs describe Ragas as a library for systematic evaluation loops, LLM-app metrics, test set generation, and integrations with frameworks such as LangChain and LlamaIndex.

sources.title: DeepEval 5-min Quickstart
sources.note: Official quickstart shows local evaluation through test cases, metrics, and the deepeval test run workflow, with optional Confident AI reporting.

sources.title: Phoenix: Evaluate RAG
sources.note: Official Phoenix cookbook demonstrates building a RAG pipeline, tracing it, and evaluating retrieval and response quality with Phoenix Evals.

sources.title: LangSmith: Evaluate a RAG application
sources.note: Official LangSmith tutorial covers datasets, running a RAG app over examples, and metrics such as correctness, relevance, groundedness, and retrieval relevance.

sources.title: Promptfoo: Evaluating RAG pipelines
sources.note: Official guide frames RAG evaluation as separate retrieval and generation checks, with output-based, context-based, and custom metrics usable from CLI/config workflows.

findings.text: Ragas should be considered a RAG-focused evaluation framework candidate because its official docs emphasize systematic LLM-app eval loops, custom and built-in metrics, test set generation, and common framework integrations.

DeepEval is a strong CI/regression-testing candidate: its official quickstart is structured around local test cases, evaluation metrics, and a deepeval test run command, with optional hosted reporting through Confident AI.
Phoenix belongs in the observability-plus-evaluation category because the RAG cookbook combines tracing/instrumentation with retrieval and response evaluation.

LangSmith represents a managed experiment/dataset workflow for RAG evaluation: the official tutorial organizes evaluation around datasets, running the app on those datasets, and measuring answer relevance, accuracy, and retrieval quality.
Promptfoo is a lightweight CLI/config-oriented candidate for production teams that want to test retrieval and generation separately and wire RAG checks into repeatable automation.

A useful XLSX scoring model should likely separate tool type, license/hosting posture, RAG-specific metric coverage, CI automation, trace/observability support, dataset/testset support, human feedback/annotation, integration footprint, operational maturity, and evidence quality.

The current source set is a seed shortlist, not yet enough for final comparison. It still needs coverage of Langfuse, Opik, TruLens, MLflow GenAI, Giskard, Evidently, Weave, LlamaIndex, Haystack, and OpenAI Evals before synthesis.

adequacy.goal_alignment: Adequacy was not evaluated because this was the first search iteration. The evidence gathered aligns with the goal by starting a public-source tooling matrix, but it is not yet sufficient for finalization.
adequacy.coverage_summary: Covered five official sources across RAG-specific eval frameworks, CI-style eval testing, observability/evaluation, managed experiment workflows, and CLI/config-based RAG testing. Major candidate families remain uncovered.

covered_requirements.requirement: Use public, current, official or vendor/GitHub sources.
covered_requirements.evidence: All five captured sources are official documentation pages from the tools or vendors.

covered_requirements.requirement: Shape work toward a decision matrix for production RAG evaluation tooling.
covered_requirements.evidence: Findings extract practical positioning and initial scoring dimensions relevant to an XLSX matrix.

coverage_gaps.gap: Need official evidence for remaining commercially relevant and open-source tools before comparing options.
Need license, hosting, pricing/public tier, and GitHub maturity checks for open-source/self-hosted claims.
Need to decide which tools are primary rows versus exclusions or caveats.

evidence_risks.risk: Vendor docs are authoritative for features but may overstate operational maturity or omit trade-offs; later iterations should corroborate with GitHub repos, changelogs, and integration docs.

adequacy.recommended_next_angle: Gather official docs/GitHub evidence for the remaining candidate tools and normalize them into a compact candidate list with inclusion/exclusion rationale.

validation_evidence.reason: No private identifiers, local-only tool names, personal data, or scraped contact data were included in the findings.
The iteration used official documentation pages rather than blog summaries.
The research is only a seed shortlist and not yet broad enough for a final decision matrix.
